notes: Chapter argument reconstruction: Chapter 35 argues that Roman Catholicism's essential character and aims are unchanged despite a rising 19th-century Protestant mood of conciliation, and that the restraints of secular liberty alone (not any change of heart) prevent a revival of persecution. The chapter builds this case in three movements: (1) documented papal statements against liberty of conscience and the episcopal oath of obedience (solidly supported, establishing Rome's own self-testimony); (2) a psychological/theological account of why ceremonial religion and confession attract the 'unrenewed heart' and why Rome's historical cruelty reflects satanic deception (mostly unverifiable theological framing); (3) an extended historical excursus (Constantine's edict, Eusebius, medieval Sunday legislation, the forged 'Sunday letter,' Trent, the Waldenses, and the Ethiopian church) establishing that Sunday observance is Rome's manufactured institution and 'the sign of her authority' -- the load-bearing historical premise that lets the book's climactic Sabbath-vs-Sunday final-test thesis (Revelation 13, mark of the beast, ch36ff.) attach to a concrete institutional villain. The chapter closes by arguing Rome's Gregorian/Innocentian claims to depose rulers persist in principle and would be exercised again given the power. Impact-stage notes: 0 confirmed discrepancies after defense (the sole anachronistic item, GC-35-039, was reversed to supported and is not scored). Per orchestrator instruction, the 7 disputed items carried through the defense pass were scored under the high-stakes-disputed rule; all other claims (unverifiable/theological/interpretive framing, and supported factual/quotation claims) are left without an impact object, consistent with the confirmed-discrepancy-only default. GC-35-049's task-note grouping with the 'Sunday origin with Rome' cluster does not match its actual content (the heretics-and-faith maxim, not Sunday origin), so it was scored independently on its own substance rather than folded into the Sabbath/Sunday cluster.

verification.rationale: The sweeping claim that Catholic laity as a body were denied Scripture is overstated by modern scholarship. Deanesly's Lollard Bible documents that prohibitions of vernacular Bible reading were regional and targeted (Toulouse 1229, Tarragona 1233, imperial bans), not a universal ecclesiastical ban; the Cambridge History of the Bible states no pope or council ever issued a universal prohibition, and Wim François notes vernacular biblical books circulated widely. verification.rationale: The image of church officials systematically engineering maximal, indefinitely repeated torture reflects the Protestant martyrological tradition (Foxe, and White's sources Wylie and Dowling). Modern revisionist scholarship (Kamen, Peters) documents that inquisitorial torture was in fact regulated: it could normally be applied only once, for limited duration, was barred from causing bloodshed, mutilation or death, required medical supervision, and a confession had to be freely repeated afterward to be valid. Torture undeniably occurred (Lea), but the systematic-maximization characterization is contradicted by modern historiography while matching 19th-century sources. source_quality: academic-partial (JAAPL, Wikipedia revision; Lea).

defense.argument: The verifier's own academic source undercuts its verdict on the two checkable cores of White's sentence. (1) 'The infernal process was repeated to the utmost limit of human endurance.' The verifier's central rebuttal — that torture 'could normally be applied only once' — is precisely the rule Lea documents as a legal fiction routinely circumvented. Lea vol. 1 (line 14587): 'According to rule, torture could be applied but once, but this, like all other rules for the protection of the accused, was easily evaded,' with 'the next or third day appointed for renewal of the infliction.' Lea vol. 3 (line 16614): 'Though, as formerly, torture could not be repeated, it could be "continued" indefinitely, with prolonged periods of intervening imprisonment in dungeons of which the squalor was purposely heightened to exhaust the mental and physical forces of the victim.' That is a near-paraphrase of White's 'repeated to the utmost limit of human endurance, until nature gave up the struggle.' So the 'single application' limit corroborates rather than contradicts the claim. (2) 'To cause the greatest possible torture, and not end the life of their victim.' The verifier lists the no-bloodshed / no-death / physician-present rules as a refutation, but these are the very content of White's clause: torture was deliberately calibrated to stop short of death precisely to preserve the victim for a valid, repeatable confession. White asserts non-lethal design; the revisionist procedure confirms non-lethal design. The verifier read its own evidence as opposing a claim it actually supports. The extractor's neutral claim_text ('devised methods to inflict maximum torture without ending their lives, sometimes repeating the process until the victim died from exhaustion') is supported clause by clause: 'devised methods' — Wylie vol. 2 (line 22256), 'a most ingenious but cruel apparatus had been devised' for 'prolonged and excruciating tortures'; 'without ending their lives' — the no-death rule; 'sometimes repeating until the victim died from exhaustion' — Lea's 'continued indefinitely... to exhaust the... forces of the victim,' with deaths under torture attested though formally against the rules. Tracing to White's period sources (Wylie/Foxe) is confirmed. The one genuinely black-legend element the verifier flagged — imputing sadistic maximization as the institution's conscious purpose ('studied, under Satan their master, to invent means') — is homiletic/theological framing ('under Satan their master'), not a falsifiable procedural proposition, and the extractor's claim_text does not carry it. The revisionists (Kamen, Peters) temper the FREQUENCY and centrality of torture in inquisitorial practice, not the reality of the prolongable, calibrated, non-lethal cruelty this claim describes. On the neutral checkable proposition, modern historiography (Lea, the verifier's own academic source) supports the claim; verdict should move from anachronistic to supported. source_quality: academic-full (Lea vols. 1 & 3, local; Wylie vol. 2 for period tracing).

claims.quote: The churchyard contains millions of victims, who spent their lives in vain endeavors to subdue their natural affections, to repress, as offensive to God, every thought and feeling of sympathy with their fellow-creatures.

verification.rationale: Refers to monastic/ascetic self-mortification rather than to killings. The ascetic ideal of renouncing natural affections was widespread across many centuries of monasticism, so 'millions' over the medieval period is a defensible hyperbole; the framing is rhetorical/interpretive rather than a precise statistic.
verification.modern_consensus: Ascetic renunciation was a pervasive feature of medieval monastic life.

claims.quote: Such was the fate of Rome’s opponents. For her adherents she had the discipline of the scourge, of famishing hunger, of bodily austerities in every conceivable, heart-sickening form.

claims.quote: If we desire to understand the determined cruelty of Satan, manifested for hundreds of years, not among those who never heard of God, but in the very heart and throughout the extent of Christendom, we have only to look at the history of Romanism.

claims.quote: The pope claims to be the vicar of Christ; but how does his character bear comparison with that of our Saviour?

claims.quote: “Faith ought not to be kept with heretics, nor persons suspected of heresy,”(1006) she declares.

verification.rationale: The maxim is popularly attributed to the Council of Constance (in connection with Hus's broken safe-conduct) and appears in Protestant polemic as Catholic doctrine. Catholic historians (Hefele, Pallavicino, cited in the Catholic Encyclopedia) deny that the council ever decreed that faith need not be kept with heretics; what Constance actually held was that a secular safe-conduct did not bar the Church from trying and punishing a heretic. Genuine historiographical disagreement over attribution and meaning.
verification.modern_consensus: The Council of Constance did not formally decree the maxim as doctrine; it ruled that an imperial safe-conduct did not protect Hus from ecclesiastical judgment.

sources.title: Council of Constance (Catholic Encyclopedia)

sources.title: Council of Constance
sources.note: Safe-conduct vs. heresy trial; disputed maxim.

impact.reasoning: The maxim 'faith need not be kept with heretics' is popularly attached to Constance/Huss, but Catholic historians deny it was ever formally decreed as doctrine; what Constance actually held is narrower (a secular safe-conduct does not bar ecclesiastical trial of a heretic). This is an overstated attribution rather than an invented event, so severity is moderate. It functions in paragraph 28 as one more rhetorical brick in the chapter's 'Rome is perfidious and unchanging' argument, but that argument is built mainly of well-supported material elsewhere in the chapter (Dictatus Papae, the episcopal oath, Pius IX's encyclicals); removing this one contested maxim leaves the paragraph's point essentially intact.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A fact failure specific to tense: the 1415 Constance decree (heretics and suspects lose safe-conduct protection) is well-supported and the quoted words substantively track it, but the ruling finds improbable the printed present-tense attribution that the Church still declares this as standing 1911 doctrine, since mainstream Catholic theology from Becanus (1607) onward, and the Catholic Encyclopedia (1908), explicitly denied the maxim. The framing_gap of 3 shows real recovery: the weakened claim (an unrepealed but historically bounded conciliar precedent) is well-supported, so the failure is temporal over-extension dressed in the chapter's 'chameleon/serpent invariance' rhetoric, not fabrication of the underlying episode. Centrality is moderate: paragraph 28 is one of several rhetorical bricks (alongside the independently well-supported Dictatus Papae quotations and episcopal oath) building the 'Rome unchanging' case, so its removal weakens color without collapsing the argument. Independent failure point, no chain inheritance.

claims.quote: the principal object contemplated is the enforcement of Sunday observance,—a custom which originated with Rome, and which she claims as the sign of her authority.
claims.claim_text: Sunday observance as a religious custom originated with the Roman Catholic Church, which claims it as a sign of her authority.

verification.rationale: Two parts. That the Church claims the Sabbath-to-Sunday change as a mark of her authority is well supported (e.g., Archbishop del Fosso at Trent 1562; Catholic catechetical statements). That Sunday observance 'originated with Rome' is genuinely contested: Bacchiocchi (Pontifical Gregorian UP) argues the Church of Rome played a decisive role in institutionalizing Sunday in the 2nd century, but the mainstream (Rordorf; Carson-edited volume; Catholic scholarship) traces Sunday worship to earlier, broader apostolic-era resurrection practice not specifically Roman in origin. source_quality: academic-full (Bacchiocchi + Vaida).
verification.modern_consensus: Sunday worship predates any Roman legal enforcement and arose broadly in the early church; Rome's specific originating role is debated (Bacchiocchi vs. Rordorf/Carson).

sources.title: S. Bacchiocchi, From Sabbath to Sunday, ch. 6-8 (Pontifical Gregorian UP, 1977)
sources.note: Argues Roman role in origin of Sunday; represents the minority academic position.

sources.title: Did the Early Church Move the Sabbath...? (mainstream summary)
sources.note: Mainstream/Catholic position: Sunday from apostolic resurrection practice, cited as a position.

impact.reasoning: That Sunday observance 'originated with Rome' is a live scholarly dispute: Bacchiocchi (a minority but credentialed academic position) argues for a decisive 2nd-century Roman institutional role, while the mainstream traces Sunday worship to broader, non-specifically-Roman apostolic-era practice -- not fabrication, but a genuinely contested causal claim. Centrality is maximal because this single sentence is the hinge on which paragraphs 35-51 (more than a third of the chapter) turn: the entire Constantine-Eusebius-medieval-legend-Trent excursus exists to establish that Sunday is Rome's manufactured institution, the direct historical premise for the book's central Sabbath-vs-Sunday final-test thesis (mark of the beast, ch36ff.). Delete this claim and the chapter loses the causal bridge connecting 'aims of the papacy' to the book's eschatological climax; the ensuing forty paragraphs of historical illustration would then support a premise the text itself treats as merely contested. Load-bearing both for the chapter's own structure and for the book's central thesis chain.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: This is the chapter's hinge claim, and the fact failure is substantive: the earliest unambiguous first-day witnesses (Barnabas, Ignatius, the Didache) are geographically dispersed and predate or run independent of Rome, so 'originated with Rome' is ruled improbable even after full engagement with the strongest published minority case (Bacchiocchi, Pontifical Gregorian UP 1977). The framing_gap is only 2, the smallest in this batch: the weakened version ('Rome played a leading role in demoting the Sabbath and promoting Sunday') recovers only to probable, not well-supported, so the causal-origin claim loses real ground even after the most charitable reconstruction. Centrality is maximal: paragraphs 35-51 (the Constantine-Eusebius-medieval-legend-Trent excursus, over a third of the chapter) exist to cement this sentence's premise, the direct historical bridge to the book's climactic mark-of-the-beast thesis; delete it and forty paragraphs of illustration support a premise the adjudication itself now finds does not hold as stated. Load-bearing for the chapter's own argument; independent failure, no chain inheritance.

verification.rationale: Constantine's edict of 3 March 321 (Codex Justinianus III.12.3) is standardly identified as the first civil legislation enforcing rest on Sunday. Confirmed by Bacchiocchi (academic) and printed in Ayer's Source Book. source_quality: academic-full.
verification.modern_consensus: Constantine's 321 law is the first imperial Sunday-rest legislation.

verification.rationale: Ayer's Source Book prints the law verbatim: 'All judges and city people and the craftsmen shall rest upon the venerable Day of the Sun. Country people, however, may freely attend to the cultivation of the fields.' Exact match. source_quality: academic-full (primary text).
verification.modern_consensus: The 321 law's wording (venerable Day of the Sun; farmers exempted) is uncontested.

The 'virtually heathen statute' characterization is grounded in the edict's solar framing. source_quality: academic-full.

claims.quote: A zealous advocate of Sunday, who about the close of the twelfth century visited the churches of England, was resisted by faithful witnesses for the truth; and so fruitless were his efforts that he departed from the country for a season, and cast about him for some means to enforce his teachings.

verification.rationale: White follows Andrews, who reports that Eustace brought the roll 'from the pope,' implying a papal-curia origin. But the 'Letter fallen from Heaven' (Himmelsbrief/Sunday Letter) is a much older apocryphal text circulating from at least the 6th century across the Latin and Greek worlds; its ultimate origin is disputed and not specifically the medieval papal palace. The attribution of authorship to 'the pontifical palace at Rome' is the source's inference rather than established fact.
verification.modern_consensus: The Sunday Letter is an early apocryphal 'Letter from Heaven' of debated (non-papal-curial) origin, predating the Eustace episode by centuries.

sources.title: Eustace of Flay / Sunday Letter tradition
sources.note: Older, non-papal apocryphal origin of the Letter from Heaven.

impact.reasoning: The specific inference that the forged 'Letter from Heaven' Eustace of Flay carried came from 'the pontifical palace at Rome' traces to Andrews's own inference rather than an established fact; the Sunday Letter is a much older apocryphal tradition of debated, non-specifically-papal origin. This is a low-severity embellishment of one illustrative anecdote among many. Centrality is low because this detail is decorative color within the larger Sunday-origin argument, which rests on Constantine, Eusebius, and the Trent quotation (GC-35-057, 059-063, 085), not on the precise authorship of one 12th-century forged letter; deleting it leaves the broader medieval-legend narrative and the chapter's argument entirely unaffected.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A narrow fact failure: no documentary basis ties the 1201 'Letter from Heaven' roll's production to the pontifical palace -- the contemporary chronicler Roger of Howden records Eustace of Flay's campaign with no papal chain, and the apocryphon itself predates the episode by six centuries. This hardens Andrews's own inference ('responsibility... directly to the pope') into a claim of physical origin the sources do not support, but the framing_gap of 3 shows the underlying phenomenon (a human clerical forgery exploited to enforce Sunday) remains well-supported once the papal-palace specificity is dropped. Centrality is low: the chapter's broader Sunday-origin argument rests on Constantine's edict, Eusebius, and the Trent quotation, not on the precise findspot of one twelfth-century forged letter, so removing this clause leaves the chapter's argument fully intact. Independent evidential failure, unrelated to any other claim's failure.

verification.rationale: The roll's prohibition of labor from the ninth hour (3 p.m.) Saturday to Monday sunrise, backed by reported miracles, is exactly as recorded by Hoveden and quoted by Andrews. The report exists in the source.

verification.rationale: Andrews quotes Morer: at a council in Scotland under Innocent III (A.D. 1203), King William passed into law that 'Saturday from twelve at noon ought to be accounted holy,' with worldly business forbidden until Monday morning. The unnamed 'king of Scotland' is William the Lion; the quotation matches.
verification.modern_consensus: Early Scottish Sabbath legislation extended holy time from Saturday noon to Monday.

claims.quote: In the sixteenth century a papal council plainly declared: “Let all Christians remember that the seventh day was consecrated by God, and hath been received and observed, not only by the Jews, but by all others who pretend to worship God; though we Christians have changed their Sabbath into the Lord’s day.”(1014)
claims.claim_text: A papal council in the sixteenth century declared: 'Let all Christians remember that the seventh day was consecrated by God, and hath been received and observed, not only by the Jews, but by all others who pretend to worship God; though we Christians have changed their Sabbath into the Lord's day.'

verification.rationale: The 'sixteenth-century papal council' is the Council of Trent, where Archbishop Gaspare (Caspar) del Fosso of Reggio (address of 18 January 1562) openly declared that the Church, by its own authority, changed the Sabbath into the Lord's day. The substance—a Tridentine prelate affirming the church-authored transfer of the seventh-day Sabbath to Sunday—is confirmed; the exact wording White gives is transmitted through her 19th-century source. source_quality: academic-partial.
verification.modern_consensus: Del Fosso's Trent address asserting the church's authority to change the Sabbath to Sunday is a documented 16th-century statement.

claims.quote: A striking illustration of Rome’s policy toward those who disagree with her was given in the long and bloody persecution of the Waldenses, some of whom were observers of the Sabbath.
claims.claim_text: The Roman Catholic Church conducted a long and bloody persecution of the Waldenses, some of whom observed the Sabbath (seventh day).

verification.rationale: The long, bloody persecution of the Waldenses by Rome is uncontested. The subordinate claim that 'some of whom were observers of the Sabbath' (seventh day) is genuinely disputed: some early modern writers (Goldast, the inquisitor Pegne) linked the label 'insabbatati/inzabbatati' to Sabbath-keeping 'in the manner of the Jews,' but mainstream Waldensian historians (Comba, Spini, Audisio) derive the term from the preachers' distinctive shoes (sabots) or from generic 'witches'-sabbath' heresy charges, and hold the Waldenses were Sunday-keepers. The seventh-day claim is a minority/contested reading.
verification.modern_consensus: Waldensian persecution is fact; seventh-day Sabbath observance among them is rejected by mainstream scholarship though defended by a minority tradition.

impact.reasoning: That 'some' Waldenses were seventh-day Sabbath-keepers rests on a minority reading of the 'insabbatati' label (Goldast, Pegne) against the mainstream derivation from distinctive footwear or generic heresy-charge vocabulary (Comba, Audisio); the persecution itself is uncontested, but the Sabbatarian identification is a contested overlay. Centrality is elevated because Sabbatarian continuity through the medieval underground churches recurs across the book as a pillar of the argument that the true Sabbath was never entirely extinguished, feeding directly into the final Sabbath-vs-Sunday thesis (cf. ch26's parallel, more load-bearing claim). Within this chapter specifically, however, the claim is a single passing clause introducing paragraph 46 before the argument's weight shifts to the far more extensively documented Abyssinian Sabbath-continuity narrative (¶46-47); removing the Waldensian aside leaves that stronger illustration, and the chapter's 'Rome persecutes Sabbath-keepers' point, intact.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: The persecution of the Waldenses and its use as an illustration of Rome's coercive policy both stand as well-supported; the fact failure is isolated to the existential claim that some Waldenses kept the seventh-day Sabbath, which specialist etymology (insabbatati as footwear, Hoose 2016) and the documented festival-rejecting-but-Sunday-keeping Waldensian record push to improbable. The framing_gap is the smallest in this batch (1): even the narrowed weakened claim (a marginal Bohemian judaizing group, not Waldensians as such) reaches only genuinely-open, resting on one unengaged 15th-century catalog entry, so recovery is thin rather than substantial. Within the chapter this is a single introductory clause before the argument's weight shifts to the far more extensively documented Abyssinian Sabbath-continuity narrative (paragraphs 46-47), so its removal leaves the chapter's 'Rome persecutes Sabbath-keepers' point intact; centrality is nonetheless elevated because Sabbatarian continuity through hidden medieval communities is a recurring pillar feeding the book's central Sabbath-vs-Sunday thesis. Independent failure point; not chain-inherited.

claims.quote: Amid the gloom of the Dark Ages, the Christians of Central Africa were lost sight of and forgotten by the world, and for many centuries they enjoyed freedom in the exercise of their faith.
claims.claim_text: During the medieval period, the Christians of Central Africa (Ethiopia/Abyssinia) were largely isolated from and forgotten by the wider world, enjoying freedom of religious practice for many centuries.

verification.rationale: The Christian kingdom of Ethiopia/Abyssinia was largely cut off from European Christendom through much of the medieval period and practiced its faith autonomously, as Andrews (following Gibbon) and modern scholarship on the Ethiopian church attest. 'Central Africa' is a geographical imprecision (Ethiopia is in the Horn/East Africa; Andrews wrote 'interior of Africa'), but the substance is accurate.
verification.modern_consensus: Ethiopian Christianity developed in prolonged isolation from Latin/Greek Christendom.

Andrews cites the prohibition of Saturday observance under penalty; Gibbon likewise ('the Abyssinians were enjoined to work and to play on the Sabbath'). Consistent with the historical record.
verification.modern_consensus: The Jesuit reforms suppressed Ethiopian Saturday-Sabbath observance.

verification.rationale: Ethiopian Christianity's observance of both Saturday (Sabbath) and Sunday is well documented; the practice of the two 'Sabbaths' (Sanbat) was deeply rooted. Accurate.

But the polemical equation of modern Protestant Sunday advocacy with medieval 'fabricated miracles' is an interpretive comparison, not a checkable equivalence, and is contestable.

impact.reasoning: That contemporary Protestant Sunday-advocacy rests on 'the same lack of scriptural evidence' as medieval papal leaders is a real and widely acknowledged point (no explicit New Testament command transfers the Sabbath to Sunday), but the polemical equation with the papacy's 'fabricated miracles' is an interpretive analogy rather than a checkable equivalence, so the verdict properly stays disputed rather than confirmed. Centrality is high because this sentence is the direct rhetorical bridge from the chapter's historical Sunday-law narrative to its climactic prophetic claim that these 'scenes are to be repeated' when Protestants and Catholics unite -- the exact mechanism the book's eschatological chapters (36ff.) depend on. It is not load-bearing, though, because the surrounding prophetic argument (¶48-50) is framed as theological/unverifiable interpretation of Revelation 13 independent of this specific historical analogy; deleting it weakens the rhetorical color but not the chapter's prophetic claim as such.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: The fact failure is narrow and specific: the anaphoric claim that 'papal leaders... fabricated miracles' to manufacture Sunday's authority has no documentary support, the same evidentiary gap already found in GC-35-076's pontifical-palace claim, since both rest on hardening Andrews's inference about the same forged roll. The other components underlying this sentence (Protestant divine-authority claims, the genuine absence of scriptural warrant, and the shared-evidentiary-gap frame) are well-supported or probable, and the framing_gap of 3 confirms the weakened comparison ('like medieval promoters, Protestants claimed divine authority without a command') is well-supported once the fabrication anaphora is dropped. Centrality is elevated despite the narrow defect because this sentence is the chapter's rhetorical bridge from the historical Sunday-law narrative into paragraph 51's prophetic climax, though it is not load-bearing since the surrounding Revelation-13 argument is framed as theological interpretation independent of this specific historical analogy. Independent evidential failure, not chain-inherited from GC-35-076 despite the shared underlying source problem.

claims.claim_text: The doctrinal/political principles associated with Pope Gregory VII and Pope Innocent III remained the principles of the Roman Catholic Church as of the early twentieth century.

verification.rationale: A polemical continuity claim. It has partial grounding: by 1911 the church had not officially repudiated broad church-over-state principles, and the Syllabus of Errors (1864) reasserted the church's coercive prerogatives and rejected liberal separation of church and state. But the specific medieval claim to depose rulers and release subjects from allegiance was effectively defunct as practical policy, and modern historiography would not treat the Gregorian/Innocentian program as the operative principles of the modern church. Genuine disagreement over whether these remained 'the principles' of the church.
verification.modern_consensus: The church retained maximalist church-state doctrine into the 19th century (Syllabus) but no longer asserted the practical deposing power of Gregory VII/Innocent III.

sources.title: Syllabus of Errors (1864)
sources.note: 19th-century reassertion of church prerogatives over the state.

sources.title: Dictatus papae (Gregorian principles)
sources.note: The medieval deposing power was no longer operative policy by 1911.

impact.reasoning: The claim that 'the principles of Gregory VII and Innocent III are still the principles of the Roman Catholic Church' has partial grounding (the Syllabus of Errors, 1864, reasserted maximalist church-over-state doctrine) but conflates persisting doctrinal claims with the specific medieval deposing power, which had become practically defunct by 1911 -- an overstatement of continuity rather than invention. Centrality to the chapter is very high since this sentence essentially restates the chapter's own thesis (Rome's character and aims are unchanged); centrality to the book's broader thesis chain is more moderate, since the 'Rome never changes' motif is a supporting premise for, not identical with, the Reformation-Millerite-sanctuary-Sabbath chain itself. Not load-bearing, because the chapter already establishes Rome's persisting infallibility claim and coercive doctrine independently and more solidly (GC-35-005, GC-35-006, GC-35-011, GC-35-015 are all supported), making this specific Gregory-VII/Innocent-III formulation redundant support rather than the argument's sole foundation.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: The ruling shows a tied fact-and-frame failure: the political strand (deposing power as an operative 1911 principle) fails as fact -- Vatican I defined nothing on temporal power, Leo XIII framed two distinct supreme orders, no pope of the era asserted deposition -- while the totalizing 'she never changes' identity fails as frame by collapsing real ecclesiological continuity (infallibility, supremacy) together with a defunct political program. The framing_gap of 3 shows real recovery: the weakened claim (Rome never repudiated the medieval claims and reasserted church-over-state doctrine via the Syllabus and Immortale Dei) is well-supported, so the as-written failure is an overstatement of continuity, not invention. Centrality is high because the sentence essentially restates the chapter's own 'aims of the papacy' thesis, but it is not load-bearing: the chapter already establishes Rome's persisting infallibility and coercive doctrine through independently supported material (the Dictatus Papae quotations, the episcopal oath, Pius IX's encyclicals), making this specific Gregory-VII/Innocent-III formulation redundant rather than foundational. Independent evidential failure, no chain inheritance.

claims.quote: And had she but the power, she would put them in practice with as much vigor now as in past centuries.
claims.claim_text: The Roman Catholic Church, if it had the power, would put the principles of Gregory VII and Innocent III into practice with as much vigor as in past centuries.

verification.rationale: Counterfactual prediction about institutional intent; not testable.

claims.quote: God’s word has given warning of the impending danger; let this be unheeded, and the Protestant world will learn what the purposes of Rome really are, only when it is too late to escape the snare.
claims.claim_text: Scripture has given warning of an impending danger, and if unheeded, the Protestant world will discover Rome's true purposes only when it is too late to escape.

verification.rationale: Theological warning/prediction; unverifiable by definition.
